INTRODUCTION

Investigating officers should, whenever possible, follow the mandate, investigate then interview rather than interview then investigate.

This manual provides information on the Conversation Management Model. It is designed to develop knowledge and understanding of the model to assist officers to conduct effective suspect interviews.

Some researchers consider there is no difference between the style of interview required for a witness and that required for a suspect. There are many similarities, particularly in relation to structure, but an interview with a suspect is usually conducted in an entirely different atmosphere and within a vastly different legal framework. For this reason, the two are dealt with separately.

The success or otherwise of an interview with a suspect should not be measured by proof of a suspect’s guilt or whether the suspect makes some form of admission. The aim of the interview is to discover whether the suspect was involved in the matter for which they are being interviewed and to obtain accurate and reliable information. It is essential that every effort be made to obtain an account from the suspect. The purpose of an interview with a suspect is no different to that with a witness: to ascertain the truth of the matter under investigation.

Interviewers must always enter an interview with an open mind and remain that way throughout the interview. At the same time, interviewers must not accept the account at face value, but rather must listen carefully and objectively to what is said. The account must be probed at the appropriate time and subsequently compared to what is already known at that time, to help determine truth and accuracy. Interviewers should be constantly aware of the dangers of not listening to the suspect and only hearing what the interviewer expects or wants to hear. A good interviewer listens to all that is said, disregarding information only when the interviewer is satisfied it is irrelevant, but still keeping it at the back of the mind in case it later becomes relevant1.

This resource will either refresh your understanding of investigative interviewing, or provide you with the foundations if the concepts are new to you.

Related Information

More information on investigative interviewing can be found on the Investigative Standards Unit Intranet Site (Investigative Interviewing page).

1 Investigative Interviewing Explained 4th edition LexisNexis Butterworths.

DEFINITIONS

To assist officers, a definition of terms used in this manual is shown below.

Audiovisual Record of Interview (AVROI): Recording of an interview by means of DVD or video or other electronic method.

Automated Interview Plan (AIP): Electronic interview plan used for suspects and witnesses. Located on the WA Police Force Intranet here .

Challenge: Seeking an explanation from the interviewee for inconsistencies between facts known by police and the interviewees account.

Confession: An admission or part admission, or any inculpatory statement, whether communicated verbally, in writing, or any other form.

Conversation Management Model: A structured interview model used to interview a suspect.

Culturally and Linguistically Diverse (CaLD): A person who is vulnerable due to their culture or whose second language is English.

Interviewee: The person who is being interviewed.

Investigating Officer (IO): The officer who is investigating the incident.

Investigative interviewing: The questioning of witnesses and suspects to obtain accurate and reliable information about matters under investigation.

Investigatively important topics: Of value to the investigation because it helps to establish the nature and elements of the offence, possible defences, the identity of the offender or further lines of enquiry.

PEACE Model: An interviewing framework that provides a structure to planning and conducting effective investigative interviews.

Physical Material (PM): Items which include DNA, fingerprints, impressions, photographs, clothing, weapons, documents, computer generated data, trace evidence, Closed Circuit Television (CCTV) footage, ballistics, crash reports and all other forensic samples.

Suspect: A person suspected of having committed an offence, whether or not he or she has been charged with the offence.

TEDS: A mnemonic that is helpful when constructing open questions. It stands for: Tell, Explain, Describe, Show.

The Five Key Investigative Strategies (The5KIS): The5KIS are strategies designed to capture, categorise, manage and evaluate all potential evidence relevant to an investigation.

The5KIS are categorised into Physical Material, Witnesses, Intelligence, Public Awareness and Suspects.

Vulnerable Person: For the purpose of a police interview, be that suspect or witness, is defined as someone who falls within one or more of the following categories:

• A child is always a vulnerable person.

• People with impaired intellectual function are always vulnerable.

Or people who:

• Have impaired physical functioning.

• Are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islanders.

• Are from non-English speaking backgrounds.

• Have suffered an emotional or traumatic incident.

Which as a result, may:

• Predispose them to suggestibility.

• Impact on voluntariness.

• Complicate communications between the police and the interviewee.

Any of the above circumstances may result in flawed information being captured during the interview.

Persons fitting this definition must be afforded enhanced services to ensure fairness to the person and accuracy of the information obtained during an interview.

5W&H: A mnemonic that is helpful when constructing open questions. It stands for Who, What, When, Where, Why and How.

sections.h:
Definition of a Suspect

A person suspected of having committed an offence, whether or not he or she has been charged with the offence2.

PEACE Model for Suspects

The PEACE Model is the framework utilised by WA Police Force to conduct suspect interviews.

Planning and Preparation

Prepare and plan for the interview using the Five Key Investigative Strategies (The5KIS).

Identify if the suspect is vulnerable and prepare prior to interview. For example, during the planning phase the need to consider Anunga Guidelines or a responsible adult is identified.

Engage and Explain

Build rapport, explain the interview process, and cover legislative, policy and case law obligations.

Account, Clarify and Challenge

Using the Conversation Management Model gain an account of events, clarify and challenge any inconsistencies. Account for new information.

Closure

Conclude the interview as per the Audiovisual Record of Interview (AVROI) guide. Discuss future state with the suspect. For example, procedures to be undertaken if the suspect is charged, future court appearances etc.

Evaluate

Evaluate how the information obtained impacts on the investigation and also evaluate the performance of the interviewer.

2 Section 115 Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA).

The Conversation Management Model is the model utilised to interview suspects. The steps to conduct a Conversation Management interview are outlined below. The Conversation Management Model will be covered fully in this manual.

sections.h:
Planning is an essential element of a good interview. At the earliest stage of planning, any vulnerabilities of a suspect must be identified and accommodated. (Refer Vulnerable Suspect section).

The detail of an interview plan may vary depending upon the overall circumstances. The successful outcome of an interview is often determined by the level of thought and preparation applied before the interview. The interview plan is a documented record of the objectives to be covered during the interview, incorporating The5KIS and all factual, legal and procedural matters that need to be addressed. Topics to be covered should be listed.

An interview plan is not a thought, it is a written plan to assist you throughout the interview.

An interview plan can be located at Investigative Standards intranet site. The essence is to investigate then interview – not interview then investigate.3 Consider these areas in your planning and preparation for interviewing a suspect:

Interview objectives (The5KIS).

Investigatively important topics (Police Agenda).

Production of Physical Material (PM) during AVROI (include any documentation, i.e.

maps, photographs etc, or any PM to be shown to the suspect).

Suspect profile: includes criminal history, associations, family circumstances, employment, etc.

Legal requirements (Legislative, policy or case law obligations).

Practical arrangements (Security of suspect, interview location etc) Consider breaks during the interview. During a suspect interview it also may be beneficial to break up the interview into multiple interviews. For example, after completely exhausting the suspects account of an alleged offence, before commencing the Police Agenda.

Interview Objectives

Examine all available information, witness statements, scene examination, PM, Closed Circuit Television (CCTV) footage and other supporting documents. Review witness statements/reports to assist in your written plan. Set the objectives for the interview (include covering identified investigatively important topics using The5KIS, For example, elements of offence, suspect's movements at relevant times, forensic material linking the suspect to the crime scene, For example, blood, DNA material, fingerprints, clothes).

Written Interview Plans

Police Agenda – Investigatively important topics

3 Investigative Interviewing Explained 4th Edition Brian Ord, Gary Shaw, Tracey Green A written interview plan:

Summarises the interviewer's objective(s) and provides a framework on which to base questioning;

Assists you to:

• Keep track of what's been covered and what remains to be dealt with;

• Identify areas where the suspect’s account conflicts with what is already known or

has been suggested in other accounts; and

• Identify new information while keeping track of the interview's objectives.

Gives you confidence and flexibility to conduct a professional and effective interview.

Should include:

• Your opening question. “What was your involvement in……………….”

• What is known and what needs to be established; and

• The investigatively important topics (Police Agenda) you plan to cover; evidence,

element/s of the offence/s and probable defences. Decide how to cover these during the interview.

Identify the geographical area of the offence. It is an advantage to physically examine the scene.

Introduce PM.

Identify possible challenges and in what order they are to be delivered.

Decide what allegations, if any, to outline to the suspect if they adopt their right to silence.

Demonstrate that police considered legal, policy and case law obligations before and during an AVROI.

Contain any other relevant points.

You may need to change or add to these points during the interview as the suspect introduces new information requiring clarification or challenging.

Officers are encouraged to use the Automated Interview Plan (AIP) when interviewing suspects even when not mandatory to do so. It allows the interviewer to produce an interview plan tailored to the suspects vulnerabilities that need to be addressed during interview.

The completed AIP will include legislative, policy and procedural requirements that need to be met to ensure a legally defensible interview is conducted.

The AIP is located here.

Determine the Roles of the Interviewers

Unless exceptional circumstances exist, a suspect will be interviewed by two police officers;

the lead interviewer and the corroborating interviewer. It should be decided during the planning stage as to who the lead is and who the corroborator is. It is vitally important that interviewers understand their respective roles and maintain the required discipline to keep to their designated role4.

Status of a Person to be Interviewed

Prior to interview, it is important to establish the status of person, to ensure they are afforded the correct rights.

Interviewees are defined as:

• Arrested suspect.

• Suspect / accompanying person.

Arrested Suspect

Definition: There is a reasonable suspicion the person has committed the offence under investigation and been arrested on suspicion for that offence.

Consideration must be given at this stage to the manner in which you are going to effect the arrest. The way in which the suspect is arrested will impact on their demeanour, cooperation and willingness to speak.

An arrested suspect must be given Section 137 & 138 Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (WA)

(CIA) rights. Record the time, date and the suspect’s responses when the rights are given.

During this process, you will be able to identify if the arrested suspect is vulnerable and requires further considerations.

Investigators need to be aware that to comply with Section 118 CIA any admissions or confessions made by an adult suspect for an indictable offence that cannot be dealt with by a court of summary jurisdiction, or any admissions made by a child for an indictable offence, irrespective of whether, if an adult were charged with it, it could be dealt with by a court of summary jurisdiction must be audiovisually recorded5 to be admissible in court.

Investigators will keep accurate records of all interactions with the suspect. If the suspect makes an immediate denial or admission ensure the denial or admission is covered during your planning phase and addressed during the subsequent AVROI.

Investigators need to develop rapport with the suspect (continue talking to them about unrelated topics prior to the AVROI). Ensure that you keep a brief record outlining what was discussed.

It is important to consider any circumstances that may make the suspect vulnerable and ensure the suspect’s vulnerability is catered for during your planning. Ensure the interview is conducted accordingly. For example; Juvenile, Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander, interpreter required, Anunga Guidelines, responsible adult etc.

Accompanying Person / Suspect

If an Investigating Officer (IO) identifies a suspect during an investigation where the level of suspicion has not reached the burden of reasonable suspicion, the IO can interview the person as a suspect. Give the suspect Section 28 CIA rights and with the exception of advising the suspect they are under arrest, give Section 137 & 138 CIA rights. During this time, assess if the person is a vulnerable person.

If at any time you reach a reasonable suspicion of the suspect you must immediately arrest the person on suspicion for the offence and confirm that they understand their rights under Section 137 & 138 CIA. Allow them the opportunity to exercise the rights if they wish to do so.

Vulnerable Suspects

It is vital that all officers understand the different characteristics that may lead to a suspect being classified as a vulnerable suspect and understand the steps that need to be undertaken to ensure the vulnerable suspect is interviewed appropriately.

Use of the AIP will assist in identifying and addressing the suspects vulnerabilities, ensuring the interview is conducted appropriately.

Definition of a Vulnerable Person

Vulnerable Person: For the purpose of a police interview, be that suspect or witness, is defined as someone who falls within one or more of the following categories:

• A child is always a vulnerable person.

• People with impaired intellectual function are always vulnerable.

Or people who:

• Have impaired physical functioning.

• Are Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islanders.

• Are from non-English speaking backgrounds.

• Have suffered an emotional or traumatic incident.

Which as a result, may:

• Predispose them to suggestibility.

• Impact on voluntariness.

• Complicate communications between the police and the interviewee.

Any of the above circumstances may result in flawed information being captured during the interview.

Persons fitting this definition must be afforded enhanced services to ensure fairness to the person and accuracy of the information obtained during an interview.

The following are important considerations that will impact on what provisions need to be made relating to suspects. Decisions on who will conduct the interview, the structure, style, duration, and pace of the interview will be made based on information relevant to these issues. As the investigating officer (IO) it is paramount any factors effecting the vulnerability of a suspect are identified and dealt with in line with legislation, policy and case law. If suspects are not interviewed appropriately, vital information may be missed, contaminated or not accepted by the court.

While it is not possible to supply an exhaustive list of people who may be vulnerable in the criminal justice system, the following list will assist in identifying vulnerability factors:

Child / juvenile.

Aboriginal people and Torres Strait Islanders

Immaturity, either in terms of age or development.

Any infirmity, including early dementia or disease.

Mental illness.

Intellectual disability

Illiteracy or limited education which may impair a person’s capacity to understand police questions.

People who are of non-English speaking background.

Alcoholism.

Physical disabilities including deafness or loss of sight.

Drug dependence.

Cultural, ethnic or religious factors including those relating to gender attitudes.

Trauma.

It must be understood that a person’s vulnerability does not have to be permanent. Suspects need to be assessed in accordance with how they present to police at the time of contact and should be dealt with appropriately.

Any suspect identified as vulnerable must be dealt with in a manner that ensures fairness.

Whilst it is widely thought that the Anunga Guidelines relate specifically to Aboriginal or Torres Strait Island people, the principles contained in the guidelines should be considered when dealing with any person identified as vulnerable.

Juvenile / Child Suspects

Prior to interviewing a juvenile there are a number of legislative, policy and case law obligations to be met. As a minimum, and before interviewing a juvenile, police should be familiar with the below references:

Police Manual JV-01.01.17 Procedure for Interview of Juveniles JWRL (a child) –v- The State of WA Case Law citing considerations for the interview [2009] WASC 285 of a Juvenile

Section 20 Young Offenders Act 1994 Legislation citing obligations when interviewing a

(WA) Juvenile Offender

Section 29 Criminal Code A person under the age of 14 years is not criminally responsible for an act or omission,

unless it is proved that at the time of doing the act

or making the omission he had capacity to know

that he ought not to do the act or make the

omission.

Rebutting the presumption:

IO’s may use the following indicators to rebut the presumption with the following:

• Electronic Record of Interview (EROI);

• Home background and school life;

• The ‘more obviously heinous’ the offence,

• the easier to rebut the presumption;

• Circumstances surrounding the offence

• which can prove capacity;

• Evidence of normality;

• Previous record;

• Statements by the child to police; or

• Statement made by the child during or after the offence

AVROI evidence is the most preferable form of evidence as it comes directly from the child and refers directly to the child’s appreciation of the act. A child must be cautioned and afforded their rights under the Criminal Investigation Act 2006 prior to being questioned in relation to their capacity in connection with a suspected offence IO’s avoid leading questions and yes/no answers. A child should be encouraged to provide reasons for their answers and explore the dimension of moral reasoning.

There is a 3-Stage general approach in AVROI’s to cover capacity. The case of R v El (2009) QCA 177 sets out a line of hypothetical questioning which can be used in AVROI’s to prove capacity.

Stage 1 – involves family level questions in order to establish the child knows right or wrong in a moral sense. IO’s may choose the use the following questions (or similar) as a guideline:

• If you get into trouble at home – if you bully your little sister/brother and yell at them.

Is that the right thing or wrong thing? Why? What would your mum/dad do?

• If you don’t clean your room and make your room messy. Is that right or wrong? Why?

What would your mum/dad do?

Stage 2 – involves asking school level questions to determine that the child knows right or wrong in a moral sense at a higher level. IO's may choose the use the following questions (or similar) as a guideline:

• If you get into trouble in school – so if you do something naughty at school like

push/hit your classmate for no reason. Is that right or wrong? Why? What would your teacher do?

Stage 3 – involves real world questions to determine if the child knows what is lawful and unlawful. IOs may choose the use the following questions (or similar) as a guideline:

• Do you know what is stealing / stealing a motor vehicle / robbery / burglary? Is it right

or wrong to steal other people’s things?

• Is it right or wrong to break into someone’s home? Why? Would Police be involved?

• What will be the punishment or consequence if Police get involved?

If the Prosecution can successfully establish all 3 stages of questioning – they have the evidence to prove the child not only has the capacity to know what is right or wrong, but also know what is lawful and unlawful– this can help to establish the burden of proof, beyond reasonable doubt.

Australian Courts appear to be more open to receiving evidence of a previous finding of guilt by a child.6 It has been held that it is admissible to establish capacity, even though it is not allowed under similar circumstances for an adult offender.

There is no set formula for evidence sufficient to rebut the presumption, it will depend on the circumstances of each case. The best approach for IO’s is to collect as much evidence as possible, to assess whether the Prosecution can prove capacity beyond reasonable doubt.

Who can be an Independent Person

• An adult, other than a member of the police, who is present with the consent of the

person responsible for the young person (if the responsible person can be found).

• In the case of a young person who is of or above the age of 16 years, an adult who

was present at the consent of the young person.

• A legal practitioner of the young person's own choosing (if the young person is over

16 years of age).

• A person who has not been involved in the investigation of the offence and has no

interest in the outcome of the investigation and who is present during the interview and who is approved by the child.

Police officers should endeavour to identify responsible persons within their district who may be used as independent persons when required. Where practicable, persons of the same sex and similar ethnic, cultural and/or religious background to the young person should be utilised.

NB: To protect their independent judicial status, Justices of the Peace will not be used.

A member of the WA Police Force (sworn or unsworn) cannot be used as an independent person during an interview, unless the member is the parent or guardian of the young person, or is nominated by the parent or guardian of the young person. Adult Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander Suspect

Suspect Who is Culturally and Linguistically Diverse

(CaLD) or English is Their Second Language

To interview an adult who does not understand, speak or communicate in English as a first language the interviewing officer must comply with Sections 10, 137 and 138 CIA. The interviewing officer shall utilise an approved interpreter or translator as outlined in the Police Manual.

Practical Arrangements

Decide:

• Whether the suspect is a vulnerable suspect.

• Who should conduct the interview and who should corroborate.

• Who should be present during the interview (For example, interpreter, responsible

adult, interview friend; etc).

• Where and when the interview should take place. (For example, is the suspect in

prison)?

Examine the interview room and arrange seating to ensure all people in the interview room are visible. (Usually in the ten to two position if only the suspect and interviewing officers present).

Check equipment. Make sure the equipment is functional.

Have communication aids ready including pens and paper.

Prepare PM to be presented during the interview.

Decide whether you want the interview monitored by a supervisor, colleague etc. and make necessary arrangements.

Consider what is likely to happen after the interview. For example; arrest, medical examination, photographs, fingerprints, DNA, returned home.

Safety Considerations

Suspects being interviewed may pose a danger to your safety and others in the office, as well as to the security of police property and information. Ensure that:

The suspect is always searched prior to the interview.

If the suspect has a history of violence or displays violent or unstable behaviour, a plan must be put in place to ensure officer safety during the interview.

A designated and secure interview room is used wherever available and is searched prior to the start of the interview to remove any items that could be used as a weapon.

A second officer is present or nearby to ensure security of the suspect.

If an officer feels it is unsafe to interview an arrested suspect do not conduct the interview, officer safety is paramount.

The suspect is not left unattended and is accompanied at all times.

Visitors, including legal advisers, are not left unattended while on police premises, other than when in private consultation with their client.

If Suspect Elects to Speak to a Legal Practitioner

On advising the suspect that they can communicate with a legal practitioner, you must provide them with appropriate means to facilitate this. If there is a genuine belief that by allowing the suspect to communicate with a legal practitioner (or another person) an ongoing investigation may be jeopardised you may suspend this right see Section 138(4) CIA.

Ensure this decision is recorded and outline the reasons.

If they do not have a legal practitioner or their legal practitioner is unavailable, the suspect should not be interviewed until they have an opportunity to communicate with a legal practitioner. (The legal practitioner does not need to be “their” legal practitioner or a legal practitioner nominated by the suspect).

Allow the suspect to speak to the legal practitioner and receive instruction.

Allow the suspect to speak to the legal practitioner in private.

If the suspect chooses not to communicate with a legal practitioner proceed with the AVROI after completing your interview plan.

If a person cannot contact their legal practitioner and does not wish to participate in an AVROI until they speak to a legal practitioner, they can be lawfully detained pursuant to Section 141(g) CIA until they speak to a legal practitioner.

Note Taking

It is important to remember that the suspect’s interview is going to be recorded. It is not necessary to take comprehensive notes. Limited notes should be taken by the lead interviewing officer to identify topics raised by the suspect and to assist their memory when summarising the information obtained from the suspect. The lead interviewer should 9 McKinney v The Queen (1991) 171 CLR 468

maintain good interaction with the suspect including body language and actively listening.

More comprehensive notes should be taken by the corroborating interviewer to assist their recollection of information obtained to assist them in identifying questions possibly missed by the interviewing officer on each topic. Notes made of the suspect’s answers to investigatively important topics will be used to assist in formulating effective challenges.

‘Notes made at the time’ by police comprise documentary evidence. Authorship is always important when presenting documentary evidence in court. Notes made at the time should be signed and dated by the officer making the notes and countersigned and dated by the corroborating officer. Any notes made are disclosable and will be retained in the CRIME file.

Decide whether you want the interview monitored by a supervisor, colleague etc. and make necessary arrangements. Consider what is likely to happen after the interview. For example; arrest, medical examination, photographs, fingerprints, DNA, returned home. Safety Considerations Suspects being interviewed may pose a danger to your safety and others in the office, as well as to the security of police property and information. Ensure that: The suspect is always searched prior to the interview. If the suspect has a history of violence or displays violent or unstable behaviour, a plan must be put in place to ensure officer safety during the interview. A designated and secure interview room is used wherever available and is searched prior to the start of the interview to remove any items that could be used as a weapon. A second officer is present or nearby to ensure security of the suspect. If an officer feels it is unsafe to interview an arrested suspect do not conduct the interview, officer safety is paramount. The suspect is not left unattended and is accompanied at all times. Visitors, including legal advisers, are not left unattended while on police premises, other than when in private consultation with their client. If Suspect Elects to Speak to a Legal Practitioner On advising the suspect that they can communicate with a legal practitioner, you must provide them with appropriate means to facilitate this. If there is a genuine belief that by allowing the suspect to communicate with a legal practitioner (or another person) an ongoing investigation may be jeopardised you may suspend this right see Section 138(4) CIA. Ensure this decision is recorded and outline the reasons. If they do not have a legal practitioner or their legal practitioner is unavailable, the suspect should not be interviewed until they have an opportunity to communicate with a legal practitioner. (The legal practitioner does not need to be “their” legal practitioner or a legal practitioner nominated by the suspect). Allow the suspect to speak to the legal practitioner and receive instruction. Allow the suspect to speak to the legal practitioner in private. If the suspect chooses not to communicate with a legal practitioner proceed with the AVROI after completing your interview plan. If a person cannot contact their legal practitioner and does not wish to participate in an AVROI until they speak to a legal practitioner, they can be lawfully detained pursuant to Section 141(g) CIA until they speak to a legal practitioner. No Pre-Interview Interviews9 Do not conduct interviews off-record. Start AVROI as MCKINNEY DIRECTION: soon as practicable. Ensure that you have planned Officers are reminded of the McKinney and are prepared to conduct the AVROI with the warning which can be issued to a jury in suspect. Never engage in pre-interview interviews the case of uncorroborated and disputed confessions: “It is inherently dangerous (engaging the suspect in an interview off camera in to convict on the uncorroborated an attempt to gain admissions before the AVROI). disputed confessions of the accused to Judges view these interviews as highly inappropriate police as police are considered and there is a high likelihood of your pre-interview professional witnesses”. interview and subsequent AVROI being ruled inadmissible. Note Taking It is important to remember that the suspect’s interview is going to be recorded. It is not necessary to take comprehensive notes. Limited notes should be taken by the lead interviewing officer to identify topics raised by the suspect and to assist their memory when summarising the information obtained from the suspect. The lead interviewer should 9 McKinney v The Queen (1991) 171 CLR 468 maintain good interaction with the suspect including body language and actively listening. More comprehensive notes should be taken by the corroborating interviewer to assist their recollection of information obtained to assist them in identifying questions possibly missed by the interviewing officer on each topic. Notes made of the suspect’s answers to investigatively important topics will be used to assist in formulating effective challenges. ‘Notes made at the time’ by police comprise documentary evidence. Authorship is always important when presenting documentary evidence in court. Notes made at the time should be signed and dated by the officer making the notes and countersigned and dated by the corroborating officer. Any notes made are disclosable and will be retained in the CRIME file.

sections.h:
The Engage and Explain phase starts when you commence the AVROI.

Ask the suspect the questions from the AVROI guide and record their responses.

Explain the reason for the interview as outlined in the AVROI guide. Provide the suspect with their appropriate CIA rights including the caution. Ensure the suspect fully understands the rights and has been given the opportunity to exercise them. A simple yes they understand is not sufficient, you must be satisfied (and be able to satisfy a court) the suspect fully understands their rights. Break the rights and caution down into sections and ask the suspect to explain their understanding of each part.

Importance of Listening

During the interview you must actively listen to the suspect and consider how their account relates to the evidence.

sections.h:
Suspect Account

At the completion of Engage and Explain, the Interviewers first question should be:

• “What is your involvement in………………..…….... (the offence under investigation)?”

Do not ask the suspect, “What do you know about the offence?” The subtle difference between asking a person to describe their involvement as opposed to what they know about an offence is important. Police seek to determine the suspect’s involvement in the offence.

The suspect may know about the offence from having been told something or read something in the media. The question, “What is your involvement in…?” is specific and designed for this purpose.

Questions to be asked at the commencement of the interview:

• If the suspect admits the offence………………………… “Tell me what happened”

• If the suspect denies the offence………………………“Tell me what you were doing

between [the relevant time span, day and date of the offence under investigation.]”

For example; “Tell me what you were doing between 10am and 12pm last Tuesday 16 September 2016 in as much detail as you can.”

If the offence you are interviewing in relation to is a historic offence, (For example, the suspect does not remember the day of the offence), you may wish to proceed straight to the Police Agenda (your interview plan) and ask questions relevant to investigatively important topics.

If the suspect is willing to give an account then police will use the Conversation Management Model and ask for a suspect account. Use the following guidelines to obtain the suspect account:

Obtain an account by use of an open question.

Note down topics for further questioning.

Summarise the suspect’s initial account.

Identify topics from suspects initial account.

Question the suspect on each topic using TEDS and 5W&H questions.

Pass to the corroborator at the end of each topic.

When the corroborator finishes asking questions on the identified topic, you can either summarise what has been said by the suspect or ask more questions on the topic and then summarise.

Move on to the next topic.

The below diagram summarises the method of questioning when obtaining the suspect account.

Summarising

When interviewing suspects, summarising should be used by the lead interviewer at the end of each topic to lock the suspect into their account and emphasise important aspects of their account to the suspect and anyone watching the interview.

Police Agenda

Use the following guide to obtain responses during the Police Agenda:

Identify topics from your plan (investigatively important topics) that have not been explored during the suspect account.

Question the suspect on each topic using TEDS and 5W&H questions.

Pass to the corroborator at the end of each topic.

When the corroborator finishes asking questions on the identified topic summarise what has been said by the suspect before moving to the next topic.

Ensure you have questioned the suspect on all investigatively important topics before you move to the challenge phase.

The below diagram summarises the method of questioning when obtaining the Police Agenda.

Production of Physical Material During AVROI

All PM to be used during an interview should be taken into the interview room. If the PM is too large or dangerous, photographs of the PM should be taken and produced at the relevant point during the interview. If possible do not allow the suspect to view the PM / photographs prior to questioning them on the relevant topic.

All PM to be shown to the suspect during the interview should be produced during the Police Agenda phase of the AVROI.

The exception to this is if the suspect discusses the topic during the suspects account. If this occurs produce the PM as below. Ask all relevant questions relating to the PM as you would have during the Police Agenda.

There are a number of ways PM can be produced during an AVROI:

• The PM can be shown to the suspect before any questions relating to it are asked.

Show the PM to the camera, describe the PM and any relevant reference numbers.

Ask relevant questions pertaining to the PM. Remember to cross to the corroborator to ascertain if they have any relevant questions to ask. Ensure the security of the PM is maintained at all times.

• The second method is to commence asking the suspect questions relating to the PM.

When several questions have been asked, produce the PM as described in example one. Continue to ask relevant questions relating to the PM until all questions are exhausted. Remember to cross to the corroborator to ascertain if they have any relevant questions to ask. Ensure the security of the PM is maintained at all times.

• The third method is to question the suspect prior to showing them the PM. When the

interviewer is satisfied the suspect has been asked all relevant questions cross to the corroborator to ascertain if they have any questions to ask. Show the PM to the suspect as described in example one. Ensure the security of the PM is maintained at all times.

Prior to the challenge phase all questions relevant to the PM should be asked of the suspect either during the Suspects Account or the Police Agenda.

For example; if the suspects DNA is recovered from 1 Smith Street, Joondalup, the suspect must be questioned as to whether he/she has ever been to 1 Smith Street, Joondalup before the challenge phase. Cover all aspects of the suspect’s involvement, if any, with 1 Smith Street, Joondalup. This will limit their ability to change their story when the challenge of their DNA being at the address is given.

You do not have to accept the first answer given. Avoid excessive repetitive questioning on the same point. However, re-phrasing the question and being robust at times in demanding an explanation is acceptable. There is no prescriptive number of times a question can be asked. Remember a magistrate or judge will decide whether your questioning is oppressive or unfair.

Check your plan and ensure you have covered the relevant 5KIS, elements of the offence, probable defences, mitigating circumstances and motives. If the interview is being monitored take a break and check with the monitor whether there are any more questions that need to be asked.

Challenge

The challenge phase of the interview should never be made prematurely. Ensure you have clarified the suspect account and police agenda before challenging the account provided.

The challenge phase of the interview is used when police have reason to believe the suspect is withholding relevant information or is knowingly giving a false account. The purpose of this phase is to compare what police know against the suspect’s account.

Use the following guide to obtain responses during the Challenge phase of the interview:

Must be after all topics are exhausted.

Must have previously been questioned on the topic of the challenge.

Order of challenges - weakest to strongest.

Adopt a clarifying approach.

Ask for an explanation.

Inconsistencies between the suspect's account and the evidence should always be challenged.

Follow these steps to challenge evidence:

• If there are inconsistencies between what the suspect said and the evidence, you

should challenge them by seeking an explanation.

• Before challenging the suspect consider having a short break in the interview to

review your challenges to ensure they are still relevant and in the correct order.

Let the suspect know what you are doing, for example:

• "I just need to consider what you have said to me and look over the information I

have."

Structure the challenges so each is dealt with individually and you present the challenges from weakest to strongest. For example:

• Challenge 1: inconsistencies within the suspect's account and known information.

• Challenge 2: inconsistencies between the suspect's account and witness information.

• Challenge 3: inconsistencies between the suspect's account and forensic material.

Introduce each challenge by seeking an explanation following this process:

• You said............... state the suspect's version of what he/she said.

• We have………... state the evidence that contradicts their version of events.

• Explain that …….. ask them to explain inconsistencies between what they have said

and the evidence.

For example:

• "You said you know nothing about the burglary at 1 Smith Street, Joondalup on the

13 September 2016 and that you have never been to that address. We have your fingerprints inside the house. Explain that to me..."

Challenge evidence in a clear and focused way.

Present each challenge in a way the suspect can understand.

Do not express opinions. Your opinion is irrelevant, it is the evidence itself that is important.

Avoid putting suggestions to the suspect about what happened.

If there is no reply, use silence (pause for a while) and then move to the next challenge.

sections.h:
Only one interviewer to challenge.

Start with weakest challenge working up to

the strongest challenge. Ensure you have spoken

about the topic of challenge before challenging on it.

THE

CHALLENGE

Listen to response You said ………………….

Move on to next challenge We have or know ………..

Explain that to me?

If the suspect declines to answer a question then there is no account which can be compared against what police know. An investigative advantage may be gained by asking questions in relation to facts known with a view to elicit a response from the suspect.

For example; police are investigating a burglary and possess a Forensic Analysis Report identifying the suspect’s fingerprint inside 1 Smith Street, Joondalup.

Police ask, “Have you ever been to 1 Smith Street, Joondalup” and the suspect replies, “No comment”. Because the suspect has not offered any explanation, there is no inconsistency between what is known and the suspect account. Interviewers should not be put off by this response. Police should be prepared to ask a limited number of questions supported by the available potential evidence. These questions should be put to the suspect during the challenge phase of the interview.

Remain Professional

Always act professionally when challenging the suspect. Being too overbearing may render the interview inadmissible. What might seem an acceptable risk to take in the heat of the moment may be viewed as unacceptable by the court. Think of the challenge phase as presenting evidence that is inconsistent with or contradicts what the suspect said.

Do not be frightened to challenge but remember you do not have to call somebody a liar to make them realise you do not believe them. Never call a suspect a liar or say you don't believe them as this is opinion evidence and irrelevant. It may affect the admissibility of your interview if it is deemed to be oppressive or overbearing.

Put challenges calmly and clearly allowing the suspect every opportunity to understand what is being suggested and to provide an explanation. There is no need for raised voices, offensive language or demeaning behaviour.

Admissions

Admissions can be partial or full (a confession). If a suspect confesses to the offence, ask the suspect “Tell me your involvement in the….….. (The offence)”

If the suspect gives you a full and honest account of their involvement, you will not need to challenge the suspect. Ensure all the topics in the Police Agenda phase are covered and clarification of the incident obtained in full. (The challenges become corroboration of the suspect’s honest account).

If the suspect partially admits the offence, ask the suspect “Tell me your involvement in the…………….. (The offence)”

If the suspect does not give an honest account the challenges will need to be put to the suspect following the Conversation Management Model.

When Suspects Exercise Their Right to Silence

Always plan and prepare for the possibility of a suspect who exercises their right to silence.

Ensure that you plan to address elements of the offence, other investigatively important topics and any defences that may be offered.

While it is your duty as an investigator to ask questions in relation to allegations made against the suspect it must be remembered that all suspects have the right to silence. This means that if they decide not to talk to you, you cannot compel them to answer questions.

Remember that if the court deems that the suspect’s right to silence was overborn any admissions made or information gained may be deemed inadmissible10 .

10 See ARTHURS -v- THE STATE OF WESTERN AUSTRALIA [2007] WASC 209 – Whether admissions made voluntarily or as a result of persistent importunity, insistence or pressure.

Despite an initial refusal, unless the suspect is rigorously opposed to answering questions, it is your professional responsibility to attempt to interview the suspect in line with the AVROI guide and the Conversation Management Model. It is the responsibility of the interviewing officers to ensure their questioning of the suspect is reasonable and any answers given by the suspect are voluntary. Never tell the suspect that participating in an AVROI is in fairness to them.

The above principles apply unless you are interviewing a suspect to whom the Anunga Guidelines relate. Specifically, rule number eight as outlined below:

‘Should an Aboriginal seek legal assistance reasonable steps should be taken to obtain such assistance. If an Aboriginal states he does not wish to answer further questions or any questions the interrogation should not continue’.

For instance, if the suspect indicates they do not want to answer any questions the interview should be stopped immediately, however, the interviewer needs to be mindful of whether the interviewee is merely uncomfortable with that particular topic or whether they are declining to answer any questions.

Suspect Sketch

If it is relevant and the suspect agrees, ask them to draw a sketch during the interview. This could include a sketch of the location of the offence, weapon/s, route/s taken etc.

Remember, a picture speaks a thousand words. Any sketch that is produced should be signed, dated and given a title by the suspect. The interviewing officer is to countersign and date the document as it is most likely to be produced as an exhibit.

Keep the sketch available for review and reference as required throughout the interview.

Any sketches made during an interview by the interviewer or the suspect are disclosable and will be retained in the CRIME file.

Breaks During the Interview

It is not compulsory to take breaks during an interview, however, it is acceptable to take breaks if required by police or the suspect. If you have a short break during an AVROI (one officer leaves the room to get a glass of water or box of tissues for the suspect), you may leave the recording device running. If the break will be protracted, state the time of the break and request the corroborator to pause the recording on the AVROI recording device.

On completion of the break, ask the corroborator to press the record button on the AVROI recorder when the interviewer and the suspect are seated in the AVROI room. State the time the interview is recommencing and confirm with the suspect that there was no discussion relating to the matter for which he/she is being interviewed off camera. Confirm there have been no threats or promises made to the suspect during the break and remind the suspect they are still under caution.

NB: The suspect should not be left unsupervised.

Documents Used During an Interview

If documents, maps, sketches are used during an interview, ensure that the suspect signs, dates and titles the document if they are willing. The interviewing officer must also sign, date and time the documents. These documents become exhibits and will be retained in the CRIME file.

Suspect Admits Offence Part Way Through the

Challenge

If the suspect admits the offence part way through the challenges, return to the suspect account and ask. “Tell me what happened?” Conduct the interview using the Conversation Management Model.

Unrelated Information

If a suspect volunteers information unrelated to the offence for which they are being interviewed, continue with the interview and advise them their information will be discussed later.

Take care not to imply or infer that the information they have given, or may give, could have a bearing on the present interview. There must be no suggestion, implied or otherwise, that any promise or inducement has been held out to the suspect.

Once the interview is complete, the suspect is free to give any information they wish. You do not need to conduct an AVROI about information unrelated to the offence in respect of which they have been interviewed unless you are going to arrest them on suspicion for an offence for which the unrelated information relates.

Allegations of Misconduct

If during the interview, the suspect makes any allegation against you or your corroborator, do not stop the recording. Either the interviewer or corroborator will leave the AVROI room and speak to a supervisor. The supervisor will enter the AVROI room and clarify the complaint and advise the suspect the matter will be investigated. The supervisor will also ensure the suspect understands their right to speak or be silent and the suspect does not feel compelled to speak to police before the AVROI continues.

If the suspect’s complaint relates to a police officer not directly related to the AVROI, advise the suspect their complaint will be brought to the attention of your supervisor and will be investigated. Ensure the suspect understands their right to speak or be silent and they do not feel compelled to continue with the AVROI.

CLOSURE

It is important to plan the closure of an interview. This should be done in a courteous and professional manner. When the interview is clearly drawing to its close, your aim should be to:

• Review information obtained and confirm all topics have been covered by referring

to your interview plan.

• Ensure there is mutual understanding about what has taken place.

• Explain what will happen in the future.

• Facilitate a positive attitude towards providing accurate and reliable information in

the future.

• The interview should be concluded according to the AVROI guide on the

Investigative Standards Intranet site.

Once you have closed the interview you should continue to adopt an approachable and professional style. Remember this may be one of a series of interviews. You should continue to maintain a working relationship with the suspect throughout.

EVALUATE

As soon as practical after the interview, consider:

• What information has been obtained.

• How the account given fits in with other available evidence.

• Whether any action needs to be taken.

• What further enquiries need to be made?

Self- Evaluation

Self-evaluation is an important part of professional development. Through critical review of your performance you can identify areas of improvement that may lead to a more professional collection of evidence.

When practical, review your AVROI. Self-evaluate your own performance:

What did you do well?

What could you have done better?

What areas can you develop?

How will you acquire these skills?

ADVICE

For advice on investigative interviewing related matters, contact the Detective Training School or the Investigative Standards Unit.

Ensure all the topics in the Police Agenda phase are covered and clarification of the incident obtained in full. (The challenges become corroboration of the suspect’s honest account). If the suspect partially admits the offence, ask the suspect “Tell me your involvement in the…………….. (The offence)” If the suspect does not give an honest account the challenges will need to be put to the suspect following the Conversation Management Model. When Suspects Exercise Their Right to Silence Always plan and prepare for the possibility of a suspect who exercises their right to silence. Ensure that you plan to address elements of the offence, other investigatively important topics and any defences that may be offered. While it is your duty as an investigator to ask questions in relation to allegations made against the suspect it must be remembered that all suspects have the right to silence. This means that if they decide not to talk to you, you cannot compel them to answer questions. Remember that if the court deems that the suspect’s right to silence was overborn any admissions made or information gained may be deemed inadmissible10 . 10 See ARTHURS -v- THE STATE OF WESTERN AUSTRALIA [2007] WASC 209 – Whether admissions made voluntarily or as a result of persistent importunity, insistence or pressure. Despite an initial refusal, unless the suspect is rigorously opposed to answering questions, it is your professional responsibility to attempt to interview the suspect in line with the AVROI guide and the Conversation Management Model. It is the responsibility of the interviewing officers to ensure their questioning of the suspect is reasonable and any answers given by the suspect are voluntary. Never tell the suspect that participating in an AVROI is in fairness to them. The above principles apply unless you are interviewing a suspect to whom the Anunga Guidelines relate. Specifically, rule number eight as outlined below: ‘Should an Aboriginal seek legal assistance reasonable steps should be taken to obtain such assistance. If an Aboriginal states he does not wish to answer further questions or any questions the interrogation should not continue’. For instance, if the suspect indicates they do not want to answer any questions the interview should be stopped immediately, however, the interviewer needs to be mindful of whether the interviewee is merely uncomfortable with that particular topic or whether they are declining to answer any questions. Suspect Sketch If it is relevant and the suspect agrees, ask them to draw a sketch during the interview. This could include a sketch of the location of the offence, weapon/s, route/s taken etc. Remember, a picture speaks a thousand words. Any sketch that is produced should be signed, dated and given a title by the suspect. The interviewing officer is to countersign and date the document as it is most likely to be produced as an exhibit. Keep the sketch available for review and reference as required throughout the interview. Any sketches made during an interview by the interviewer or the suspect are disclosable and will be retained in the CRIME file. Breaks During the Interview It is not compulsory to take breaks during an interview, however, it is acceptable to take breaks if required by police or the suspect. If you have a short break during an AVROI (one officer leaves the room to get a glass of water or box of tissues for the suspect), you may leave the recording device running. If the break will be protracted, state the time of the break and request the corroborator to pause the recording on the AVROI recording device. On completion of the break, ask the corroborator to press the record button on the AVROI recorder when the interviewer and the suspect are seated in the AVROI room. State the time the interview is recommencing and confirm with the suspect that there was no discussion relating to the matter for which he/she is being interviewed off camera. Confirm there have been no threats or promises made to the suspect during the break and remind the suspect they are still under caution. NB: The suspect should not be left unsupervised. Documents Used During an Interview If documents, maps, sketches are used during an interview, ensure that the suspect signs, dates and titles the document if they are willing. The interviewing officer must also sign, date and time the documents. These documents become exhibits and will be retained in the CRIME file. Suspect Admits Offence Part Way Through the Challenge If the suspect admits the offence part way through the challenges, return to the suspect account and ask. “Tell me what happened?” Conduct the interview using the Conversation Management Model. Unrelated Information If a suspect volunteers information unrelated to the offence for which they are being interviewed, continue with the interview and advise them their information will be discussed later. Take care not to imply or infer that the information they have given, or may give, could have a bearing on the present interview. There must be no suggestion, implied or otherwise, that any promise or inducement has been held out to the suspect. Once the interview is complete, the suspect is free to give any information they wish. You do not need to conduct an AVROI about information unrelated to the offence in respect of which they have been interviewed unless you are going to arrest them on suspicion for an offence for which the unrelated information relates. Allegations of Misconduct If during the interview, the suspect makes any allegation against you or your corroborator, do not stop the recording. Either the interviewer or corroborator will leave the AVROI room and speak to a supervisor. The supervisor will enter the AVROI room and clarify the complaint and advise the suspect the matter will be investigated. The supervisor will also ensure the suspect understands their right to speak or be silent and the suspect does not feel compelled to speak to police before the AVROI continues. If the suspect’s complaint relates to a police officer not directly related to the AVROI, advise the suspect their complaint will be brought to the attention of your supervisor and will be investigated. Ensure the suspect understands their right to speak or be silent and they do not feel compelled to continue with the AVROI. CLOSURE It is important to plan the closure of an interview. This should be done in a courteous and professional manner. When the interview is clearly drawing to its close, your aim should be to: • Review information obtained and confirm all topics have been covered by referring to your interview plan. • Ensure there is mutual understanding about what has taken place. • Explain what will happen in the future. • Facilitate a positive attitude towards providing accurate and reliable information in the future. • The interview should be concluded according to the AVROI guide on the Investigative Standards Intranet site. Once you have closed the interview you should continue to adopt an approachable and professional style. Remember this may be one of a series of interviews. You should continue to maintain a working relationship with the suspect throughout. EVALUATE As soon as practical after the interview, consider: • What information has been obtained. • How the account given fits in with other available evidence. • Whether any action needs to be taken. • What further enquiries need to be made? Self- Evaluation Self-evaluation is an important part of professional development. Through critical review of your performance you can identify areas of improvement that may lead to a more professional collection of evidence. When practical, review your AVROI. Self-evaluate your own performance: What did you do well? What could you have done better? What areas can you develop? How will you acquire these skills? ADVICE For advice on investigative interviewing related matters, contact the Detective Training School or the Investigative Standards Unit.

sections.h:
Anunga Rules

AD-01.00 Attachment B - Anunga Rules

Judgement Delivered By Chief Justice Forster

On 30 April, 1979, Mr. Justice FORSTER handed down his reasons for rejecting typewritten records of conversations in the matter of QUEEN v Angus ANUNGA, Sandy AJAX, Clancy AJAX, TJINGUNYA, Nari WHEELER and Frankie Miller JAGAMARA in the Northern Territory Supreme Court in August, 1975. (R v Anunga (1976) 11 ALR 412) Inter alia Mr. Justice FORSTER said:

"It seems to me now, however, to be important that the Court should put on record general guidelines for the conduct of Police Officers when interrogating Aboriginal persons.

I preface this statement of guidelines by pointing out that Aboriginal people often do not understand English very well and that, even if they do understand the words, they may not understand the concepts which English phrases and sentences express. Even with the use of interpreters this problem is by no means solved. Police and legal English sometimes is not translatable into the Aboriginal language at all and there are no separate Aboriginal words for some simple words like "in", "at", "on", "by", "with", or "over'', these being suffixes added to the word they qualify. Some words may translate literally into Aboriginal language but mean something different. "Did you go into his house?" means to an English-speaking person, "did you go into the building?", but to an Aboriginal it may also mean, "Did you go within the fence surrounding the house?"

English concepts of time, number and distance are imperfectly understood if at all by Aboriginal people, many of the more primitive of whom cannot tell the time by a clock.

One frequently hears the answer "long time", which depending on the context may be minutes, hours, days, weeks, or years. In case I may be misunderstood, I should also emphasise that I am not expressing the view that Aboriginal people are any less intelligent than white people but simply that their concepts of certain things and the terms in which they are expressed may be wholly different to those of white people.

Another matter which needs to be understood is that most Aboriginal people are basically courteous and polite and will answer questions by white people in the way in which they think the questioner wants. Even if they are not courteous and polite there is the same reaction when they are dealing with an authority figure such as a policeman.

Indeed their action is probably a combination of natural politeness and their attitude to someone in authority. Some Aboriginal people find the standard caution quite bewildering, even if they understand that they do not have to answer questions, because if they do not have to answer questions, then why are the questions being asked?

Guidelines to be Followed when Interviewing Aboriginals Bearing in mind these preliminary observations which are based partly upon my own knowledge and partly by evidence I have heard in numerous cases I lay down the following guidelines. They apply, of course, to persons who are being questioned as suspects.

1. When an Aboriginal person is being interrogated as a suspect, unless he is as fluent

in English as the average white man of English descent, an interpreter able to interpret in and from the Aboriginal person's language should be present, and his assistance should be utilised whenever necessary to ensure complete and mutual understanding.

2. When an Aboriginal is being interrogated it is desirable where practicable that a

"prisoner's friend" (who may also be the interpreter) be present. The "prisoner's friend', should be someone in whom the Aboriginal has apparent confidence. He may be a mission or settlement superintendent or a member of the staff of one of these institutions who knows and is known to the Aboriginal. He may be a station owner, manager or overseer or an officer from the Department of Aboriginal Affairs. The combinations of persons and situations are variable and the categories of persons I have mentioned are not exclusive. The important thing is that the "prisoner's friend', be someone in whom the Aboriginal has confidence, by whom he will feel supported.

3. Great care should be taken in administering the caution when it is appropriate to do

so. It is simply not adequate to administer it in the usual terms and say, "Do you understand that?" or "Do you understand you do not have to answer questions?"

Interrogating Police Officers, having explained the caution in simple terms, should ask the Aboriginal to tell them what is meant by the caution, phrase by phrase, and should not proceed with the interrogation until it is clear the Aboriginal has apparent understanding of his right to remain silent. Most experienced police officers in the Territory already do this. The problem of the caution is a difficult one but the presence of a "prisoner's friend" or interpreter and adequate and simple questioning about the caution should go a long way towards solving it.

4. Great care should be taken in formulating questions so that so far as possible the

answer which is wanted or expected is not suggested in any way. Anything in the nature of cross-examination should be scrupulously avoided as answers to it have no probative value. It should be borne in mind that it is not only the wording of the question, which may suggest the answer, but also the manner and tone of voice which are used.

5. Even when an apparent frank and free confession has been obtained relating to the

commission of an offence, police should continue to investigate the matter in an endeavour to obtain proof of the commission of the offence from other sources.

Failure to do this, among other things, led to the rejection of confessional records of interview in the cases of Nari WHEELER and Frank JAGAMARA.

6. Because Aboriginal people are often nervous and ill at ease in the presence of white

authority figures like policemen, it is particularly important that they be offered a meal, if they are being interviewed in a Police Station, or in the company of Police or in custody when a meal time arrives. They should always be offered a drink of water.

They should be asked if they wish to use the lavatory if they are in the company of Police or under arrest.

7. It is particularly important that Aboriginal and other people are not interrogated when

they are disabled by illness or drunkenness or tiredness. Admissions so gained will probably be rejected by a Court. Interrogation should not continue for an unreasonably long time.

8. Should an Aboriginal seek legal assistance reasonable steps should be taken to

obtain such assistance. If an Aboriginal states he does not wish to answer further questions or any questions the interrogation should not continue.

9. When it is necessary to remove clothing for forensic examination or for the purposes

of medical examination, steps must be taken forthwith to supply substitute clothing."

sections.h:
Section 10 Criminal Investigation Act 2006

Interpreters

Criminal Investigation Act 2006 Section 10 - Informing people who do not understand English.

If under this Act [CIA] an officer is required to inform a person about any matter and the person is for any reason unable to understand or communicate in spoken English sufficiently, the officer must, if it is practical to do so in the circumstances, use an interpreter or other qualified person or other means to inform the person about the matter.

Identify the country they come from and the exact language they speak (For example, a Chinese person may speak Mandarin or Cantonese).

Arrange for an interpreter using the contracted interpreting service. Provide the interpreter with an outline of the incident and the reason for the interview.

Ascertain whether the interpreter is an appropriate person to assist. They must be:

• Able to write and speak the suspect’s language fluently.

• Impartial and independent (has no prior knowledge of the suspect or

witnesses involved in the investigation).

If they know any of the parties involved in the investigation (including the suspect), they should only be used in exceptional circumstances, i.e. no one else is available and the interview cannot be delayed. The extent of the connection should also be taken into account when making this decision. If you use an interpreter who knows the parties involved make sure you keep a record of your rationale.

Record the interpreter’s qualifications and contact details or those of their organisation.

Prepare for the interview in the usual manner.

When the interpreter arrives:

• Allow the interpreter the opportunity to brief both you and the suspect on their

professional role and how they will conduct themselves.

• If necessary, inform the interpreter their role is to interpret your questions and the

suspect's answers back to you.

• Their interpretation should be as direct as possible in 'first person'. They should not

enter into general discussion with the suspect.

Make sure the interpreter understands the caution/ rights. Instruct them to clearly explain these to the suspect to ensure the suspect understands.

Consider seeking advice from the interpreter about any communication issues that may arise.

Consider the interpreter's needs for breaks during the interview.

Using the interpreter, complete the usual Engage and Explain process as you would for any other suspect. Where appropriate inform the suspect of their caution/ rights with the aid of the interpreter.

While video recording the interview:

• Ask the interpreter to introduce themselves, explain their role, experience and

qualifications. (They may later be required to give evidence in respect of their knowledge and experience).

• Explain the interpreter's role as detailed above.

• Ensure the interpreter is clearly visible during the interview.

Do not speak in segments. Let the interpreter control the length of each segment and signal to you when to stop.

Complete the account phase using an appropriate interview model. Your questions are interpreted to the suspect in their language and their answers interpreted back to you.

For safety and impartiality reasons make sure the interpreter leaves the room with you if you take any interview breaks.

Complete the closure process as usual.

Before the interpreter leaves, ensure you have their full contact details (or that of their agency).

Conduct an interview evaluation in the usual manner.

Ensure the appropriate forms are completed to initiate payment of the interpreter.

sections.h:
Section 28 Criminal Investigation Act 2006

28. Persons accompanying officers to be informed of rights

1. An officer who requests a person who is not in lawful custody to accompany the officer

or another officer for the purposes of assisting in the investigation of an offence must inform the person and be satisfied that the person understands — a. that he or she is not under arrest; and

b. that he or she does not have to accompany the officer concerned; and c. that if he or she accompanies the officer concerned, he or she is free to leave at any time unless he or she is then under arrest.

2. Subsection (1) does not apply to or in respect of a person to whom a requirement has

been made by a police officer under the Road Traffic Act 1974 Section 66.
sections.b: Section 28 Criminal Investigation Act 2006 28. Persons accompanying officers to be informed of rights 1. An officer who requests a person who is not in lawful custody to accompany the officer or another officer for the purposes of assisting in the investigation of an offence must inform the person and be satisfied that the person understands — a. that he or she is not under arrest; and b. that he or she does not have to accompany the officer concerned; and c. that if he or she accompanies the officer concerned, he or she is free to leave at any time unless he or she is then under arrest. 2. Subsection (1) does not apply to or in respect of a person to whom a requirement has been made by a police officer under the Road Traffic Act 1974 Section 66.

sections.h:
Section 118 Criminal Investigation Act 2006

118. Admission in serious case inadmissible unless recorded

1. In this section —

admission means an admission made by a suspect to a police officer or a CCC officer, whether the admission is by spoken words or by acts or otherwise;

adult means a person who has reached 18 years of age;

child means a person who is under 18 years of age;

reasonable excuse, for the absence of an audiovisual recording of an admission, includes —

a) the admission was made when it was not practicable to make an audiovisual recording of it;

b) equipment to make an audiovisual recording of the admission could not be obtained while it was reasonable to detain the suspect;

c) the suspect did not consent to an audiovisual recording being made of the admission;

d) the equipment used to make an audiovisual recording of the admission malfunctioned.

2. This section applies in respect of a suspect who is —

a) a child charged with an indictable offence, irrespective of whether, if an adult were charged with it, it could be dealt with by a court of summary jurisdiction;

or

b) an adult charged with an indictable offence that cannot be dealt with by a court of summary jurisdiction.

3. On the trial of the suspect for the offence, evidence of any admission by the suspect

is not admissible unless —

a) the evidence is an audiovisual recording of the admission; or b) in the absence of an audiovisual recording of the admission — i. the prosecution proves, on the balance of probabilities, that there is a reasonable excuse for the absence; or

ii. the court decides otherwise under Section 155.

4. Subsection (3) does not apply to an admission by a person made before there were

reasonable grounds to suspect that he or she had committed the offence.

sections.h:
Section 137 & 138 Criminal Investigation Act 2006

137. Arrested people, rights of

1. In this section —

officer means a police officer, a public officer, or any person who holds an office with power to arrest people.

2. This section applies to a person who has been arrested by an officer, no matter under

what authority or written law.

3. The arrested person is entitled —

a) to any necessary medical treatment; and

b) to a reasonable degree of privacy from the mass media; and c) to a reasonable opportunity to communicate or to attempt to communicate with a relative or friend to inform that person of his or her whereabouts; and d) if he or she is for any reason unable to understand or communicate in spoken English sufficiently, to be assisted in doing so by an interpreter or other qualified person.

138. Arrested suspects, rights of

1. In this section —

arrested suspect means a person who is under arrest having been arrested — a) under Section 128, under an arrest warrant, or under another written law, on suspicion of having committed an offence; or

b) under the Criminal Investigation (Extra-territorial Offences) Act 1987;

officer means a police officer, a public officer, or any person who holds an office with power to arrest people.

2. In addition to the rights in Section 137 an arrested suspect is entitled —

a) to be informed of the offence for which he or she has been arrested and any other offences that he or she is suspected of having committed;

b) to be cautioned before being interviewed as a suspect;

c) to a reasonable opportunity to communicate or to attempt to communicate with a legal practitioner;

d) if he or she is for any reason unable to understand or communicate in spoken English sufficiently, not to be interviewed until the services of an interpreter or other qualified person are available.

3. The officer in charge of the investigation must, as soon as practicable after the arrest

of an arrested suspect —

a) inform the suspect of his or her rights under Section 137(3)(c) and subsection (2)(c); and

b) afford the suspect his or her other rights under Section 137 and subsection (2).

4. An officer may refuse an arrested suspect his or her right to communicate or to

attempt to communicate with a person if the officer reasonably suspects that the communication would result in —

a) an accomplice taking steps to avoid being charged; or b) evidence being concealed, disturbed or fabricated; or c) a person’s safety being endangered.

[Section 138 amended by No. 21 of 2008 Section 656(8).]
