Arrested suspects, rights of 1. In this section — arrested suspect means a person who is under arrest having been arrested — a) under Section 128, under an arrest warrant, or under another written law, on suspicion of having committed an offence; or b) under the Criminal Investigation (Extra-territorial Offences) Act 1987; officer means a police officer, a public officer, or any person who holds an office with power to arrest people. 2. In addition to the rights in Section 137 an arrested suspect is entitled — a) to be informed of the offence for which he or she has been arrested and any other offences that he or she is suspected of having committed; b) to be cautioned before being interviewed as a suspect; c) to a reasonable opportunity to communicate or to attempt to communicate with a legal practitioner; d) if he or she is for any reason unable to understand or communicate in spoken English sufficiently, not to be interviewed until the services of an interpreter or other qualified person are available. 3. The officer in charge of the investigation must, as soon as practicable after the arrest of an arrested suspect — a) inform the suspect of his or her rights under Section 137(3)(c) and subsection (2)(c); and b) afford the suspect his or her other rights under Section 137 and subsection (2). 4. An officer may refuse an arrested suspect his or her right to communicate or to attempt to communicate with a person if the officer reasonably suspects that the communication would result in — a) an accomplice taking steps to avoid being charged; or b) evidence being concealed, disturbed or fabricated; or c) a person’s safety being endangered. [Section 138 amended by No. 21 of 2008 Section 656(8).]

sections.h:
Section 29 Criminal Code (WA) Fact Sheet: Criminal

Responsibility of Children (doli incapax)
sections.b: Section 29 Criminal Code (WA) Fact Sheet: Criminal Responsibility of Children (doli incapax)

sections.h:
This factsheet is intended for the internal use of the Western Australia Police Force only and is confidential and privileged. It must not be copied and/or provided externally without consulting Legal Services.

Scope

This factsheet addresses the age of criminal responsibility under section 29 of the Criminal Code (WA) (Code), the presumption of doli incapax (latin, meaning incapable of crime) and how this presumption can be rebutted. This factsheet provides information and examples of various ways for Investigating Officers (IOs) to rebut the presumption.

Summary

It is presumed that a child between the ages of 10 and 14 cannot be criminally responsible.

However, this presumption can be rebutted if the Prosecution leads admissible evidence to persuade the court that the child had the capacity to know that their acts/omissions were morally wrong by the standards of a reasonable adult.

Further, evidence led by the Prosecution must be unequivocal and unambiguous.

The rebuttal of the presumption should be considered an element of the offence during the investigative stage and form part of the Evidence Matrix.

Section 29 of the Code states:

‘A person under the age of 10 years is not criminally responsible for any act or omission.

A person under the age of 14 years is not criminally responsible for an act or omission, unless it is proved that at the time of doing the act or making the omission he had capacity to know that he ought not to do the act or make the omission.’

The Prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt that the accused had the ‘capacity to know’ that they ‘ought not to do’ the act they did.11 The Prosecution does not have to prove that the child actually knew it was seriously wrong to do the act/omission.12 To rebut the presumption of incapacity the Prosecution is required to prove that the child had the capacity to know what they were doing was wrong at the time of the offence, instead of proving that they knew this generally.

11 RYE v State of WA [2021] WASCA 43 [44].

12 Ibid.

Seriously wrong or just naughty?

The requirement for the Prosecution to establish ‘capacity to know’ exists to prevent merely naughty children from being convicted of crimes and protect them from the subsequent consequences of convictions.

The Prosecution is to establish that the child knows their act is not just ‘naughty’ for which they would be punished at home or school, but rather something that is ‘seriously wrong’ which requires punishment by a Court.3

Rebutting the presumption

IO's may use the following indicators to rebut the presumption with the following:

• Electronic Record of Interview (EROI);

• Home background and school life;

• The ‘more obviously heinous’ the offence, the easier to rebut the presumption;

• Circumstances surrounding the offence which can prove capacity;

• Evidence of normality;

• Previous record;

• Statements by the child to police; or 3 KNL v State of WA [2023] WASCA 72[76].

• Statement made by the child during or after the offence.13

EROI

EROI evidence is the most preferable form of evidence as it comes directly from the child and refers directly to the child’s appreciation of the act.

A child must be cautioned and afforded their rights under the Criminal Investigation Act 2006 prior to being questioned in relation to their capacity in connection with a suspected offence.

IOs need to avoid leading questions and yes/no answers. A child should be encouraged to provide reasons for their answers and explore the dimension of moral reasoning.

There is a 3-Stage general approach in EROIs to cover capacity. The case of R v El (2009) QCA 177 sets out a line of hypothetical questioning which can be used in EROI’s to prove capacity.

Stage 1 – involves family level questions in order to establish the child knows right or wrong in a moral sense. IO’s may choose the use the following questions (or similar) as a guideline:

• If you get into trouble at home – if you bully your little sister/brother and yell at them. Is

that the right thing or wrong thing? Why? What would your mum/dad do?

• If you don’t clean your room and make your room messy. Is that right or wrong?

Why? What would your mum/dad do?

13 Thomas, Croft ‘Prosecuting Child Offenders: Factors Relevant to Rebutting the Presumption of Doli Incapax’ (2018) 40(3) Sydney Law Review 339.

Stage 2 – involves asking school level questions to determine that the child knows right or wrong in a moral sense at a higher level. IOs may choose the use the following questions (or similar) as a guideline:

• If you get into trouble in school – so if you do something naughty at school like push/hit

your classmate for no reason. Is that right or wrong? Why? What would your teacher do?

Stage 3 – involves real world questions to determine if the child knows what is lawful and unlawful. IO’s may choose the use the following questions (or similar) as a guideline:

• Do you know what is stealing / stealing a motor vehicle / robbery / burglary? Is it right or

wrong to steal other people’s things?

• Is it right or wrong to break into someone’s home? Why? Would Police be involved?

• What will be the punishment or consequence if Police get involved?

If the Prosecution can successfully establish all 3 stages of questioning – they have the evidence to prove the child not only has the capacity to know what is right or wrong, but also know what is lawful and unlawful – this can help to establish the burden of proof, beyond reasonable doubt.

If an EROI is not sufficient to prove capacity, consider whether the forms of evidence listed below are sufficient to meet the requirements.

Home background and school life

The home and school background that a child experiences has a significant effect on the child’s ability understand the wrongfulness of their behaviour. In these environments children learn what is right and wrong, what is acceptable and unacceptable.

IO’s can obtain statements from a teacher, social worker or a family member who know the child well and can give good evidence of capacity.

When statements are obtained from the above individuals, it is important to cover whether when they told the child of a wrongdoing, did the child acknowledge this? Even a child with low literacy/numeracy skills or a child who is unengaged at school has the capacity to understand a wrongdoing.

In RP v The Queen14, the High Court held that a child accused has the requisite understanding that performing sexual acts on his younger sibling was ‘seriously wrong’ due to observing his brother’s distress. This finding does not mean that it can be assumed that the infliction of hurt and distress means that a child accused understands that an action is seriously wrong in a direct sense, however it may point to a requisite understanding.

The ‘more obviously heinous’ the crime, the easier to rebut It was held on appeal by the House of Lords in C (A Minor) v DPP (1996) AC 1, 33 that the ‘more obviously heinous’ the offence, the easier it is to rebut the presumption. Courts must be satisfied that the act was so evidently wrong that every child should know the

14 [2016] HCA 53 [35].

wrongfulness of the act and therefore there should be no, or little need for further evidence to establish capacity.

For example, some acts such as causing damage or mischief that has turned into vandalism can be more easily aligned with naughtiness. Whereas acts of dishonesty such as stealing is more likely to be understood as seriously wrong.

However, the presumption cannot be rebutted merely as an inference from doing the act/omission, no matter how obviously wrong the act/omission may be. Therefore, IOs must seek other evidence to prove capacity remembering the burden of proof.

Circumstances surrounding act proving capacity

The established circumstances which support a finding that the child understood the wrongfulness of their actions are:

• evidence of careful planning of the crime, particularly sophisticated or devious methods

of carrying out the act – for example – whether a child had a tool on them to assist them to steal an object can establish pre-meditation of the crime;

• assertion of a false alibi;

• making effort to conceal the act or divert blame; or

• running away from police – running away can be proof of an appreciation that an act was

seriously wrong, however children may run away from police because they think they have done something naughty or because they are afraid of the police – therefore it is important to ascertain the context of running away.

Inferences drawn in from the circumstances surrounding the act alone need to be placed in the context of other evidence about the child’s mental capacity. The best approach is to obtain further forms of evidence to confirm any conclusion to be drawn from the evidence available.

Evidence of normality

For some offences, it may be argued that it was inevitable that every child of normal intelligence (between the ages of 10-14) would be expected to have the capacity to know that the act is so obviously wrong and they should not behave in that way.15 However, this expectation does not mean that the Prosecution can simply disregard the presumption and not lead any evidence.

The expectation of a child to have the capacity to understand that certain acts are wrong doesn’t affect the existence of the presumption of doli incapax. It only affects the strength of the evidence likely to be necessary

to rebut it.16

To prove that a child is a normal and ordinary child, IOs can obtain:

• evidence from the child’s parents/guardians concerning their performance at school or

behaviour generally.

15 R v LAH [2016] QCA 82 [50].

16 R v B [1997] QCA 486 (Pincus JA).

• evidence from school teachers and school reports – evidence establishing that a child is

‘normal’ can come from statements from teachers and school reports but can also include an analysis of their handwriting.

• an expert report or call an expert witness (psychiatrist or psychologist) to give evidence

on the child’s developmental state. This is possible but may not always be necessary.

Previous record

Australian Courts appear to be more open to receiving evidence of a previous finding of guilt by a child.17 It has been held that it is admissible to establish capacity, even though it is not allowed under similar circumstances for an adult offender.

This evidence is seen to be acceptable only for the purpose of ascertaining whether the child knew the act was wrong and not to establish the commission of the crime.

IO’s need to be aware that previous convictions say little for certain offences. For example, a conviction for assault does not show that the child understands the wrongfulness of forgery.

The exceptions that IO’s need to be aware of are:

• Section 22(4) of the Young Offenders Act 1994 (WA) states that where “a caution is

given any admission made by the person cautioned at or about the time the caution is given is not admissible in civil or other proceedings as evidence of any matter to which the caution refers.”

• An acceptance of responsibility under a court referred juvenile justice diversion cannot

be used in evidence against the child.

Statements by the child to the police

The presumption has been rebutted where there has been an admission by the child to police that they knew the wrongfulness of the act.18 Such an admission refers directly to a child’s appreciation of the act. It not drawn from a general analysis of the personality and behaviour of the child or generalised expectations of children. However, the use of statements by children must be approached with caution. It may be found that the child knew the consequences of the act, but did not have an appreciation that the act was seriously wrong.

The possibility must be considered that a child may not appreciate the wrongfulness of the act at the time of commission, but came to the realisation it was seriously wrong as a direct result of being questioned by the police or other adults. At this point the usefulness of statements made at a much later date is doubtful.

Statement by child during or after offence

Statements made during the commission of the offence can point to a child’s capacity. The language used by the child must be examined closely.

The WA Supreme Court in R v JA19 found that the language used by the child offender to induce the child’s victim to comply with the child offender’s demands revealed that the child 17 R v GW [2015] NSWDC 52 [17]-[22]; R v F; Ex parte A-G (Qld) [1998] QCA 97.

18 JM (a minor) v Runeckles (1984) 79 Cr App R 255.

19 [2007] ACTSC 51 [83]; KNL v State of WA [2023] WASCA 72 [127].

offender was acting out of a fantasy, rather than knowing they were engaging in serious criminal activity.

Utterances made to others, not in the context of police questioning, but after the commission of the offence can be a useful form of evidence. Particularly, if the child was boasting to others about the commission of the offence, as this can make clear that they had the capacity to understand the wrongfulness of the act.

These statements may be more reliable as they are not made in direct response to questioning by a person in a position of authority. Therefore, a child is less likely to be led to give an answer. Nevertheless, there is a danger that such statements can amount to boasting to peers but does not truly reflect an understanding of the wrongfulness of the act.

Conclusion

There is no set formula for evidence sufficient to rebut the presumption, it will depend on the circumstances of each case. The best approach for IOs is to collect as much evidence as possible, to assess whether the Prosecution can prove capacity beyond reasonable doubt.

The more indicators that are assessed by IOs, the clearer the picture is of a child’s capacity to commit a crime. Approached this way, the presumption of doli incapax provides appropriate protection for children. It allows a child to be prosecuted when the Prosecution adduces evidence that the child understood the act to be seriously wrong as opposed to mischievous or naughty. Where such evidence is not forthcoming, the child must not be prosecuted.
